items.correct: Three sections: A, contrasting B, and a return to A (ABA).
items.why: Students assume 'three sections' means three different ones. Ternary has only TWO distinct ideas, but A returns - it's the return that makes it ternary, not the section count alone.

items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.

items.correct: Rapid alternation between the written note and the note a step above.
items.why: Students confuse trill (rapid alternation between two notes) with vibrato (pitch oscillation around one note). Trills add ornamentation; vibrato adds expressive width to a sustained pitch.
The answer examiners want is: Clear words. “Loud singing” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: On a loud note, you should.
items.why: Correct answer: Pull mic away. “Move closer to mic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly how many cm should mic be from mouth?
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
